visual_features.overall_style: Conservative, information-focused layout with a light, institutional/academic feel. Visual style leans minimalist and content-first, using mostly neutral colors and simple typography. It looks like a standard WordPress theme adapted for an environmental research project, with project-specific branding mainly in the header logos and hero image rather than in the core UI styling.
visual_features.visual_hierarchy: Hierarchy is driven primarily by typography and content width. The project logo strip at the top establishes brand context, followed by a large H1 page title that clearly dominates the page. Below the title, a date stamp in smaller, lighter text provides metadata. The large banner/hero image directly underneath becomes the secondary focal point. Body text is presented in a single readable column. The right sidebar content (Search, Recent Posts) is visually subordinate due to smaller column width and lighter typographic weight. Navigation links sit above the content but are visually modest (no bold backgrounds), prioritizing the page title and main article over the nav.
visual_features.whitespace_usage: Moderate whitespace around main content and between sections. The header, page title, hero image, and body paragraphs all have clear vertical separation (approximately 16–32px). The layout is not ultra-spacious, but it avoids crowding; margins around the left content column and between sidebar widgets provide adequate breathing room. There are narrow gutters between main content and sidebar, and minimal outer margins to the browser edge, emphasizing a text-centric reading layout rather than a heavily framed design.
unique_elements: Horizontal logo strip combining multiple institutional logos (Interreg, EU, project logo) as the primary brand header
Small language selector flag icon under the project logo
Hero/banner image for specific events that visually carries the environmental/blue-water color story
Classic blog-style right sidebar with 'Recent Posts' designed in a minimal, text-only style

layout_characteristics.grid_system: Two-column fixed-width layout likely based on a 12-column grid where the main content uses roughly 8–9 columns and the sidebar 3–4 columns, centered within the viewport. Content appears constrained to a max-width of about 1100–1200px.
layout_characteristics.layout_pattern: Classic blog/article layout with full-width header, horizontal top navigation, and a right-hand sidebar. The core content is a single main column of article text with a title and hero image, accompanied by a narrow sidebar for secondary navigation and recent content.
layout_characteristics.section_organization: From top to bottom: (1) Header with institutional logos and project logo, (2) Horizontal navigation bar, (3) Main article area containing page title, date, hero image and article body, (4) Right sidebar with Search widget and Recent Posts list. Sections are clearly separated by vertical spacing and subtle dividing lines (a thin light-gray border separating the sidebar).
layout_characteristics.alignment: Overall left-aligned layout with consistent alignment to a central content column. Logos in the header are horizontally aligned left-to-right. Navigation items are baseline-aligned along a horizontal line. The main article text, headings, and hero image are left-aligned within the main content column. Sidebar widgets are also left-aligned, creating parallel vertical columns.
responsive_hints: Centered main container suggests a max-width that can be adapted to smaller viewports.
Two-column layout (content + sidebar) implies a breakpoint where sidebar would likely stack below the main content on narrow screens.
Simple text-based navigation without complex mega-menus would collapse well into a stacked or hamburger menu on mobile.
layout_characteristics.content_arrangement: Linear top-to-bottom flow inside a two-column frame. The header spans full width, followed by a single horizontal navigation bar. Beneath this, the page splits into a left main column and a right sidebar. Within the left column, content flows as: H1 title, date line, full-width hero/banner image, then body text paragraphs. The right sidebar vertically stacks a search box at the top and a 'Recent Posts' heading followed by a vertical list of post links. There are no alternating columns or complex content blocks within the article itself—it's a straightforward article layout.
layout_characteristics.space_allocation: The header and navigation occupy a relatively small vertical slice at the top (approximately 10–15% of the viewport height). The main two-column content area fills the remainder. Horizontally, the main article column takes roughly 65–70% of the available width, while the sidebar takes 30–35%. Vertical spacing between key elements (title, date, image, body) is moderately generous, with roughly one to two line-heights of space, keeping the page readable but not overly airy. The hero image is large and occupies a noticeable vertical block, serving as a strong visual anchor between meta info and the article text.
layout_characteristics.content_density: Medium content density. Text is not crammed; paragraphs have sufficient line height and spacing. However, the layout uses standard blog-like spacing rather than large editorial margins. The sidebar is somewhat denser, with compact stacking of links under 'Recent Posts' but still with enough spacing to avoid clutter. Overall, the design prioritizes efficient presentation of information while maintaining readability.
layout_characteristics.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top-left with the institutional/project logos, then moves right to scan the navigation bar. From there, attention drops to the prominent page title and then down to the hero image. After processing the hero, the eye follows the main text down the left column. Secondary attention paths divert to the right sidebar where the Search and Recent Posts list live. This creates a modified F-pattern: header/logo → nav → main title → hero image → body text, with occasional glances to the right sidebar for related content.

ui_patterns.visual_description: Buttons are minimal and flat. The visible example is the 'Search' button: a rectangular shape with sharp or very slightly rounded corners (2–3px radius at most), light-gray background (#DDDDDD) and darker gray text. No pronounced shadow; separation is by color contrast. Padding appears modest (about 8–10px vertically and 12–16px horizontally), producing a compact button that aligns with the input field height. The design is utilitarian rather than highly styled.
ui_patterns.structural_pattern: Likely implemented as an HTML <button> or <input type="submit"> grouped together with a text input inside a <form>. Visually rendered as an inline block aligned immediately to the right of the search input. Other CTAs (if added) might be <a> tags styled as buttons using common WordPress patterns.

ui_patterns.visual_description: There are no explicit card components, but content blocks (main article and sidebar widgets) behave like card-like containers. The main article sits on a white background against a slightly lighter page background, separated mainly by margins rather than visible borders. Sidebar widgets (Search, Recent Posts) are stacked sections with subtle separation: a heading, then content, sometimes with a very light top border or extra spacing to delineate them. No drop shadows; separation relies on whitespace and the column grid.
ui_patterns.structural_pattern: Structurally similar to <article> or <div class="post"> for the main content, and <aside> or <div class="widget"> wrappers for sidebar modules. Each widget contains a heading element (<h2> or <h3>) followed by lists or form elements. If cards were extended to other pages, they would likely follow this widget pattern: title + content block within a padded container.

ui_patterns.visual_description: The visible form is a simple search field. The input is a horizontally oriented rectangular field with a white background, light-gray border (#DDDDDD), and subtle inner padding (about 8px). Corners are slightly rounded or square, matching the button style. No visible icons inside the input. Labels are not inline; the field relies on placeholder text or the adjacent 'Search' button to communicate purpose. Overall density is low, with ample space above and below the input in the sidebar.
ui_patterns.structural_pattern: Typical WordPress search form: <form> containing a single <input type="search" or type="text"> and a submit control (button or <input type="submit">). Elements are likely grouped inline using simple inline or flex layout. Other forms on the site would likely follow a vertical, stacked pattern: labels above inputs or to the left, with full-width text fields within the content column.

ui_patterns.visual_description: Primary navigation is a horizontal text-based menu placed just below the logo header. Each item (e.g., 'NOR-WATER', 'Results', 'Communication', 'Events', etc.) appears as plain text links with moderate letter spacing and padding on the left and right (about 12–20px). Background is white; no tab-like boxes. Active or hovered states are likely indicated by a change in link color and possibly an underline. Dropdown capability is hinted by a small downward arrow next to 'Results'. The look is simple, flat, and unobtrusive.
ui_patterns.structural_pattern: Probably an unordered list <ul> with list items <li> each containing an <a> tag, generated by WordPress’s wp_nav_menu. Dropdowns would be nested <ul> elements within parent <li>. The menu is likely wrapped in a <nav> element. Social icons at the top-right are additional small inline links or icon fonts separate from the main nav.

ui_patterns.visual_description: The 'Recent Posts' section uses a vertical text-only list of links. Each item is a single line (or wraps to two lines for longer titles) with minimal vertical spacing (approximately 4–8px between items). There are no bullets, icons, or thumbnails—just text links. Colors use standard link color (blue) and normal weight; no borders between items. This creates a compact but readable list suited to a sidebar.
ui_patterns.structural_pattern: Implemented as a <ul> or <ol> with <li> items, each containing an <a> link. The widget heading ('Recent Posts') is an <h2> or <h3> element followed by the list. Flexbox or advanced layout is not necessary given the simple vertical stacking.

heading.visual_style: Headings (e.g., page title) appear as a classic transitional serif: moderately high contrast between thick and thin strokes, with clear serifs and a formal, editorial character. It feels traditional and academic, appropriate for research content.

body.visual_style: Body text appears as a clean, neutral sans-serif or low-contrast serif with good readability and relatively standard proportions. It is understated and functional, prioritizing legibility in paragraphs.

spacing_system.component_padding: Common padding inside content blocks and sidebar widgets appears to be around 16px (2 * 8px) on all sides, with some sections (like the main article) using 24px top/bottom.
spacing_system.section_margin: Vertical spacing between major sections (title to date, date to hero image, hero image to body text, and between widgets) is in the 16–32px range, often roughly 24px. The gutter between main content and sidebar is approximately 32px.

interaction_hints: Links (titles, nav items, recent posts) likely change color on hover (darker or more saturated blue) and may show an underline based on common WordPress themes.
Navigation items may indicate active/current page with a distinct color and/or underline to help orientation.
Form elements such as the search input probably show a darker border or subtle glow on focus.
Any dropdown menus (e.g., under 'Results') would likely appear on hover with a simple fade or immediate display, using basic CSS transitions for opacity and vertical position.
Hero images and content blocks do not suggest heavy animation; transitions are expected to be minimal and subtle to maintain the academic tone of the site.
